topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sudden appearance of the bouquet in the middle of the crowded office breakroom caused a jolt through my system. A cascade of vibrant roses and lilies materialized on the counter, heavy with a scent that was overwhelmingly sweet. My hand, halfway through stirring my lukewarm coffee, froze above the mug. I glanced around frantically, hoping no one had noticed the dramatic entrance of the flora. A few heads turned, and I could feel a flush creeping up my neck, warming my cheeks instantly. I mumbled something incoherent, quickly trying to shove the arrangement behind a stack of outdated memos, my movements jerky and overly fast. The silence that followed the brief commotion felt thick and judgmental.

[story 2]

When I picked up the offering, the sheer lack of accompanying note made my stomach clench. It was just a collection of meticulously wrapped tulips, placed squarely on my desk. I stared at the cellophane wrapping, my brow furrowing in confusion, then quickly scanned the room, searching for the source of this unsolicited gesture. My throat tightened as I realized everyone within earshot seemed to be observing my peculiar stillness. I mumbled a hasty, "Oh, um, thanks," the words sounding thin and reedy. I felt the heat radiating off my face intensify, and I instinctively wanted to melt into the beige partition wall, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The delivery arrived precisely at ten o'clock, a large, slightly unwieldy arrangement of sunflowers left by the main entrance. As I retrieved them, the silence of the hallway amplified the awkwardness. My palms started to sweat, making the paper wrapping feel slick beneath my fingers. I kept my eyes fixed on the ground, focusing intently on the scuff marks on the linoleum, avoiding any direct contact with the person who had clearly taken the time to arrange this display. When I finally turned to acknowledge the gesture, my voice came out pitched too high, a strained little squeak. I immediately looked down at my shoes, wishing desperately for the floor to open up and transport me somewhere else.

A cascade of vibrant roses and lilies materialized on the counter, heavy with a scent that was overwhelmingly sweet. My hand, halfway through stirring my lukewarm coffee, froze above the mug. I mumbled something incoherent, quickly trying to shove the arrangement behind a stack of outdated memos, my movements jerky and overly fast. The silence that followed the brief commotion felt thick and judgmental.

When I picked up the offering, the sheer lack of accompanying note made my stomach clench. It was just a collection of meticulously wrapped tulips, placed squarely on my desk. I stared at the cellophane wrapping, my brow furrowing in confusion, then quickly scanned the room, searching for the source of this unsolicited gesture. The delivery arrived precisely at ten o'clock, a large, slightly unwieldy arrangement of sunflowers left by the main entrance. When I finally turned to acknowledge the gesture, my voice came out pitched too high, a strained little squeak. I immediately looked down at my shoes, wishing desperately for the floor to open up and transport me somewhere else.
